data.title: EU expected to approve Omnicom's purchase of Interpublic on Monday - report
data.description:
The European Commission is expected to approve Omnicom's (OMC) $13 billion acquisition of Interpublic (IPG) on Monday.

The European antitrust regulator is set to give unconditional approval for the acquisition, according to traders, who cited a Capitol Forum reported that was circulating on Thursday. 

Omnicom (OMC) last month said it expects the Interpublic (IPG) deal to close in late November.

The deal received approval from the UK's antitrust regulator  in August.

In December, the companies announced that they would merge in a stock-for-stock transaction valued at $13.3 billion, creating the world’s largest advertising company.

data.title: Ross Stores GAAP EPS of $1.58 beats by $0.16, revenue of $5.6B beats by $190M
data.description:
• Ross Stores press release (ROST): FQ3 GAAP EPS of $1.58 beats by $0.16.
  • Revenue of $5.6B (+9.8% Y/Y) beats by $190M.
  • Shares +3%.

  • Fiscal 2025 Guidance

    Looking ahead, Mr. Conroy said, “We enter the holiday season with strong momentum and are well-positioned to offer a compelling merchandise assortment across all our stores. As a result, for the 13 weeks ending January 31, 2026, we are raising our comparable store sales forecast to be up 3% to 4% with earnings per share in the range of $1.77 to $1.85. This updated guidance range reflects approximately $0.03 earnings per share of unfavorable timing of packaway-related expenses that benefited the third quarter. In addition, we now expect tariff-related costs to be negligible in the fourth quarter.”

    Mr. Conroy continued, “Based on our year-to-date results and updated fourth quarter forecast, we are increasing our earnings per share guidance for fiscal 2025 to be in the range of $6.38 to $6.46, which includes approximately $0.16 per share negative impact from tariff-related costs. In addition, and as a reminder, 2024 fourth quarter and full year earnings per share of $1.79 and $6.32, respectively, included an approximate $0.14 earnings per share benefit related to the sale of a packaway facility.”

    Mr. Conroy concluded, “We are optimistic about our prospects for the holiday season, driven by our ongoing focus on delivering quality, branded merchandise at exceptional value. This approach continues to gain strong traction with the consumer, particularly in an environment of rising prices across mainstream retail. Additionally, the store and supply chain teams are well-positioned for the holiday season, and our new marketing campaigns have continued to build excitement. We believe that this multi-faceted approach will help us continue our positive momentum and enable us to capture additional market share.”

data.title: Kalshi raises $1B in new funding round, valuing prediction market at $11B - report
data.description:
Kalshi (KALSHI) has raised $1B in a new fundraise, valuing the prediction market startup at $11B, according to a media report on Thursday.

The huge round follows a Bloomberg report last month that recent funding offers from venture capital investors would value Kalshi (KALSHI) at $10B-$12B, at least double the $5B valuation set just weeks before.

The company's returning investors, including Sequoia and CapitalG, led the latest round, TechCrunch reported, citing a person familiar. Andreessen Horowitz, Paradigm, Anthos Capital and Neo are among the other investors in Kalshi.

Kalshi (KALSHI) did not immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

In October, rival prediction market Polymarket (POLYMARKET) was reportedly targeting a $15B valuation in a new round of talks with investors.

data.title: SEC dismisses civil action against SolarWinds, security officer
data.description:
The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission filed to dismiss its civil enforcement action against SolarWinds and Timothy G. Brown, the company's chief information security officer, the SEC said on Thursday.

Recall that in 2020, SolarWinds was the victim of a massive data breach, which allowed hackers to gain access to data from thousands of organizations, including numerous government agencies, for months. The company was acquired by Turn/River Capital for ~$4.4B earlier this year.

The SEC's decision to seek the dismissal is "in the exercise of its discretion," and "does not necessarily reflect the Commission’s position on any other case," the agency said.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Volatility spikes to a one month high as market sentiment shifts sour
data.description:
Market uncertainty spiked sharply on Thursday as the Volatility Index (VIX) climbed to its highest level in over a month, signaling growing investor caution.

The VIX is currently trading near 25.42, up 7.5% on the day, and briefly touched an intraday high of 28.27. Its last comparable level was back on October 17, when it peaked at 28.99. 

The sudden increase in market jitters comes in the wake of Nvidia’s (NVDA) latest earnings report, which initially sent the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) surging. The tech-heavy index rose as much as 2.5% earlier in the day before reversing course as it now trades lower by 1.3%. 

Other major U.S. benchmarks followed suit. The S&P 500 (SP500) and the Dow (DJI), which briefly traded in positive territory, ultimately have shifted lower. Adding to the risk-off sentiment was weakness in cryptocurrencies, with bitcoin (BTC-USD) falling below $87,000, marking a 5.2% drop. 

The VIX’s jump suggests traders are bracing for potential swings in both equities and alternative assets as the quarter progresses. See a link to a chart of the Vix here.

Here are some exchange-traded funds focused on volatility: (VXX), (VIXY), (VXZ), (VIXM), (UVXY), (SVXY), and (UVIX).

And here are some ETFs tied to the major market averages: (DIA), (DDM), (DOG), (DXD), (SDOW), (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), (SPXU), (QQQ), (QQQM), (TQQQ), (QID), and (SQQQ).

data.title: Eversource Energy downgraded at Mizuho after denial of water unit sale
data.description:
Eversource Energy (ES) -1.9% in Thursday's trading and down nearly 15% in the two days since Connecticut regulators rejected the company's proposal to sell its Aquarion water utility business, a setback to the utility's efforts to streamline operations and reduce debt.

Mizuho downgraded Eversource (ES) to Neutral from Outperform with a $68 price target, cut from $81, as analyst Anthony Crowdell said he "wrongly believed Connecticut regulation would improve and thought the first step would be the Aquarion transaction."

Crowdell said Aquarion was the major driver in improving Eversource's (ES) credit metrics, and the denial brings into question an anticipated Q2 2026 Connecticut Light & Power rate filing asking for a 25%-30% increase during a gubernatorial election, and likely pushes out $980 million of storm cost recovery to 2027 or later.

Even with the stock now trading at a 26% P/E discount after the previous session's selloff, Crowdell said he sees limited upside for Eversource (ES) due to the regulatory overhang, and noted the balance shee has been on Baa2 negative outlook at Moody's since January 2024.

The rejection is "a setback, as Eversource will now have to come up with an alternative to address its balance sheet goals," KeyBanc analyst Sophie Karp wrote, and the regualtor's unanimous vote "casts doubt over the improvement in Connecticut's regulatory environment, which has been negatively affecting utilities operating in the state."

data.title: NVent, Modine rated Buy at UBS on AI infrastructure; Hubbell rated Neutral
data.description:
UBS on Thursday expanded coverage of companies tied to the build-out of artificial-intelligence data-center infrastructure: nVent Electric, Modine Manufacturing. and Hubbell. The bank’s analysts argued that the group is positioned to generate operating-profit growth roughly 50% higher than the broader industrials sector over the next three years.

The initiation expands UBS’s existing Multi-Industry and Electrical Equipment research to cover the full industrial supply chain powering data-center expansion, from grid-level transmission and distribution to white-space thermal management. The note, led by analyst Amit Mehrotra in collaboration with UBS analyst Neal Burk, highlights growing capital spending on AI-driven power demand as a long-duration theme still underappreciated in industrial valuations.

UBS added nVent (NVT) to the firm’s list of “top Multi-Industry AI picks,” joining GE Vernova (GEV), Vertiv (VRT) and Eaton (ETN).

NVent (Buy): Electrical tailwinds at its back

UBS initiated nVent Electric with a Buy rating and $128 price target, emphasizing the company’s strong competitive position in electrical enclosures, heat management and liquid cooling, all segments vital to modern data-center reliability.

The firm estimates that data centers and utilities account for roughly 40% of nVent’s total revenue, a share expected to expand as AI-related investments strain power grids and accelerate the “electrification of everything.”

UBS projects double-digit annual revenue growth through 2027 and sees earnings power well above consensus. The analysts expect EPS to reach $4.69, which would represent 40% growth versus 2025 estimates and sit 3% above current Street expectations. The report also highlights nVent’s (NVT) history of bolt-on acquisitions (eight deals since 2019, adding an average 7 percentage points to annual revenue growth) as a continued source of upside.

Modine Manufacturing (Buy): Increasing focus on data centers

UBS also initiated Modine (MOD) with a Buy rating and $173 price target, citing the company’s growing presence in thermal-management systems for hyperscale and colocation data centers worldwide.

Modine’s 30% exposure to data-center cooling is expected to drive 15% average annual revenue growth through FY29, according to UBS. That growth should translate into what the note calls “much higher earnings capacity,” with sales and EPS forecasts for FY28 sitting 4% and 3% above consensus, respectively.

UBS argues that Modine’s valuation leaves room for re-rating: on the firm’s 2028 earnings forecast, the current share price implies a 14 times P/E, which the analysts describe as “a low multiple to pay for top-decile growth.”

Hubbell (Neutral): Right exposures, looking for growth

UBS initiated Hubbell (HUBB) with a Neutral rating and $450 price target, stating that while the company is well positioned for long-term demand in grid modernization and data-center electrification, its valuation already discounts much of that upside.

More than 50% of Hubbell’s (HUBB) revenue should benefit from higher spending on power-grid infrastructure and data centers. However, recent organic growth has been flat to modestly positive, hindered primarily by customer destocking, the report said.

UBS expects organic growth to recover gradually, forecasting 3% in 2025 and mid-single-digit growth beginning in 2026, but notes that these levels remain “fairly modest” relative to other AI-infrastructure names, which UBS projects to grow at a approximately 10% median rate, and roughly in line with the broader industrial sector.

Broader AI-infrastructure investment cycle

UBS argues that AI compute demand is reshaping capital spending across utilities, electrical equipment and thermal-management systems, creating a multi-year industrial cycle that extends beyond traditional data-center operators.

data.title: Tech Voices: Trump's AI executive order, toymaker pulls ChatGPT toys
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's statements, announcements, and remarks that could impact the technology sector.

  • President Trump has reportedly prepared a draft executive order aimed at undermining state laws regulating AI.

The draft EO directs the Justice Department to create an AI Litigation Task Force with the intent of challenging state AI laws and preempting them with less restrictive federal guidelines, according to CNN.

The White House told CNN that discussion about potential orders is speculative.

  • CNN also said that a teddy bear equipped with OpenAI's (OPENAI) ChatGPT 4o chatbot has been temporarily pulled from the market over concerns that it could engage in inappropriate conversations with youngsters.

Singapore-based Folo Toys pulled the bear along with its other AI-enabled toys after researchers at the US PIRG Education Fund reported they were able to dispense dangerous information, such as how to light a match, and discuss sexually explicit subjects. The company now plans to conduct an internal safety review of the toys, according to CNN.

PIRG said OpenAI has since suspended the toy company for violating its policies, CNN added.

Microsoft (MSFT) is a major investor in OpenAI.

data.title: Reaction Roundup: SA analysts, experts weigh in on September jobs report
data.description:
Wall Street's focus on Thursday was on the much-delayed September nonfarm payrolls report, which showed significantly stronger-than-expected job growth in the month. 

The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics said the economy added 119K jobs, much higher than the expected figure of 50K. However, nonfarm payroll figures for July and August saw major revisions. Moreover, the unemployment rate ticked up to 4.4% in September, the highest since October 2021.

The data complicates matters for watchers of monetary policy and the Federal Reserve. On the one hand, strong headline job growth indicates strength in the labor market. On the other, the big revisions and the tick up in the unemployment rate suggest underlying cracks.     

The strong September jobs report initially helped in spurring Wall Street to a massive open in the morning. Sentiment has soured since, and markets are now deep in the red. Here are some exchange-traded funds that track the benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500): (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:UPRO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS), and (NYSEARCA:SPXU).

See below for various reactions to the jobs data:

Mike Zaccardi, Seeking Alpha analyst since 2022: 

"It was a mixed, 'stale' September jobs report. The headline payrolls gain was the best since April, but the unemployment rate rose 0.12 percentage points to 4.44%, the highest this cycle.

Treasury yields fell, suggesting a greater chance of a Fed rate cut in December. The odds are only about 1-in-3, though. Unfortunately, we won't get the November employment report until December 16 (after the Dec. 9–10 Fed meeting). So I'll continue to watch alternative, private-sector-led jobs releases, along with any additional corporate layoff announcements.

In terms of price action, together with the Nvidia earnings, today likely marked a bit of a clearing event, potentially setting the stage for a classic Thanksgiving week rally and maybe a year-end melt-up."

Leo Nelissen, part of investing group iREIT+HOYA Capital:

"It's a clear case of a Fed that will be forced to cut due to the K-Shaped economy. On one hand, we have hyperscalers that lead the market and do not require lower rates. On the other hand, we have rising unemployment and sluggish cyclical growth, forcing the Fed to ease into a strong market.

This will likely be inflationary and improve liquidity in 2026."

Chris Lau, investing group leader of DIY Value Investing:

"September’s dramatic reversal of a 119K employment increase, up from a 4K dip in August, requires scrutiny. This is in contrast to both the Challenger and ADP reports.

The BLS revised July NFP down by 7,000 and August down by 26,000. Without October’s NFP report, investors will not get a possible downward revision for September’s data. This sets up the S&P 500 (IVV) to near 7,000, up from its current 6,750 level."

Olu Sonola, head of U.S. economic research at Fitch Ratings:

"This is the quintessential 'good news is bad news' story. The upside surprise in this report is positive, but it likely dampens prospects for a rate cut in December. As the only official jobs report the Fed will see before its next interest rate decision, it reinforces the idea of proceeding cautiously when visibility is limited.

The next inflation data arrives on the morning of the December meeting, and that release could prove more consequential than usual. The slight uptick in the unemployment rate complicates the narrative—pick your poison: stronger job growth or rising unemployment, because the good news may not be as good after all."

Elizabeth Renter, senior economist at NerdWallet:

"The Fed’s decision when they meet in December is a tricky one, but there’s a good chance that would be the case even with all of the data in-hand. With risks to both sides of their dual mandate at odds, they’re seeking clarity in an incomplete picture.

We don’t yet know when the next batch of consumer inflation data will be released, and this will be the last jobs report before their meeting. Fortunately, they will get some insight into the actual state-of-play in layoffs when the October JOLTS report is released the first day they convene."

Heather Long, chief economist at Navy Federal Credit Union: 

"While it’s encouraging that 119,000 jobs were added in September, the reality is the economy lost jobs in both June and August. There are very few industries that are hiring right now outside of healthcare and restaurants.

Overall, the job picture was even worse than many realized this summer, and it didn’t improve much in September. So far, firing has not picked up. Initial jobless claims remain low. But looking across the past three months, there are numerous industries that are now shedding jobs: mining, manufacturing, transportation and warehousing, information, finance, professional and business services. There needs to be a rebound in hiring for a healthy economy.”

Mark Hamrick, senior economic analyst at Bankrate: 

"Word that payroll growth was stronger than expected in September helps to quell recession fears. Even so, there are less than comforting signs in the slight rise in the jobless rate to 4.4%, the highest in nearly four years, and in the downward revisions to payrolls for both July and August. In retrospect, August saw a slight contraction in employment.

After a forced fast of federal data caused by the government shutdown, we’re finally able to begin to feast again. A bit on the stale side, the September jobs report was collected two months ago. It’s less than fully satisfying when it comes to understanding the current economy. The November report, due in mid-December, will offer a fresher sampling of where things really stand."

Seeking Alpha editor Liz Kiesche contributed to this story

data.title: Emerson slips after introducing 2028 financial targets, outlining vision for autonomous future
data.description:
Emerson (EMR) -2.7% in Thursday's trading, turning negative along with most stocks, after introducing 2028 financial targets and a shareholder return plan while outlining its strategic priorities for "engineering the autonomous future."

Key 2028 financial targets include $21 billion in net sales via a 5% organic compound annual growth rate, a 30% adjusted segment EBITA margin, $8.00 adjusted EPS via 10% CAGR, and $12 billion cumulative free cash flow for 2026-28.

Emerson (EMR) said it plans to return $10 billion to shareholders through stock buybacks and increased dividends through 2028, and posted a through‑the‑cycle framework for 4%-7% organic sales growth, 40% incremental margins, 10% adjusted EPS growth, and 18%-20% free cash flow margin.

The company also reiterated FY 2026 guidance provided earlier this month for net sales growth of ~5.5%, underlying sales growth of ~4%, and adjusted earnings of $6.35-$6.55/share, mostly lower than the $6.54 analyst consensus estimate, suggesting modest upside after a strong FY 2025.
